title: Post-Shock Resiliency Check
focus_questions: After the shock, did the market rebuild for continuation or repair toward balance?
Did the first pullback hold the new ground or give it back?

summary.thesis: The shock itself is not the main edge; the real edge is whether the market rebuilds under the new side or repairs after the emotional burst is over.
summary.operator_translation: Do not chase the first shock. Read the first pullback and the rebuilt-versus-repair branch after it.
summary.ai_usage_note: Use this card after bursts, sweeps, harvests, or fast releases where the next branch matters more than the initiating event.
summary.ai_brief: Environment: a burst, sweep, harvest, or release has already happened. Required evidence: post-shock hold quality, first pullback behavior, and whether the market rebuilds or repairs afterward. Do not trade: by chasing the shock itself or by calling the first pause failure too early. Real trigger: rebuilt continuation after the first pullback holds, or failure after the new ground cannot hold. Failure point: the market proves the opposite branch after the first readable retest.

when_relevant: A burst, sweep, harvest, or fast release just occurred.
The operator needs to know whether continuation still exists after the emotional phase ends.
Post-shock hold quality is more important than the original event label.
required_evidence: a clear initiating shock or burst happened first
the first pullback after the shock can be observed
there is enough post-shock behavior to tell whether the market rebuilt or repaired
confirmation_signals: the first shock completes and the next pullback becomes readable
price either holds the gained ground and re-accelerates or loses it and repairs
post-shock behavior becomes cleaner than the shock itself
invalidation_signals: the post-shock branch is still unresolved and the card is being forced too early
there is no usable first pullback or hold-quality read yet
the market clearly proves the opposite post-shock branch
no_trade_conditions: Do not chase the first shock before the first pullback is visible.
Do not auto-fade a shock only because spread widened during the event.
Do not call the first pause a failure without checking whether the new ground is still defended.

entry_archetypes.name: rebuilt continuation
entry_archetypes.description: Enter after the first pullback holds and the market rebuilds under the new side.
entry_archetypes.risk_note: Avoid if the rebuilt branch is still thin and unproven.

entry_archetypes.name: post-shock repair failure
entry_archetypes.description: Enter when the post-shock branch clearly fails and the market starts repairing back toward balance.
entry_archetypes.risk_note: Avoid if the shock still holds the gained ground and has not truly failed.

management_notes: The first pullback is usually the best diagnostic checkpoint.
If gained ground cannot hold after the shock, downgrade the continuation read quickly.
If depth and trade continue rebuilding under the new side, continuation quality improves.
review_questions: Did the operator trade the shock instead of the post-shock branch?
After the burst, did the market rebuild or repair?
Where exactly did continuation stop being obvious or start becoming obvious?
